data.title: Bank of America elevates trading chief DeMare in leadership shake-up - report
data.description:
Bank of America (NYSE:BAC) is planning to promote top executives, including the lender's trading head Jim DeMare, in a move that reshapes its leadership bench, according to a media report published Friday.

BAC shares inched down 0.1% in after-hours trading.

The potential leadership shake-up comes as longtime BofA (NYSE:BAC) CEO Brian Moynihan has signaled plans to stay on through the end of the decade, the article said. But he has not yet named any likely successors.

Bank of America (BAC) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Stellantis shelves full-size BEV truck, renames Ramcharger as Ram 1500 REV
data.description:
Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) is discontinuing development of a full-size battery-electric vehicle and renaming its Ramcharger vehicle to the now-shelved Ram 1500 REV as North American demand for full-size BEV trucks slows.

The re-named Ramcharger is a hybridized light-duty pickup which uses a gasoline generator to extend the range of its onboard battery. This extends the full driving distance of the vehicle to 690 miles, which includes 141 miles of all-electric driving. Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) plans to sell the vehicle next year.

The earlier version of the Ram 1500 REV was initially expected to go on sale in 2024 but faced multiple delays amid shifting consumer demand and high battery costs. The company planned to make the truck with two different sized batteries, eventually cancelling the 500-mile version early this year.

The decision to cancel development of the Ram 1500 REV is the latest in a series of adjustments by Stellantis’ new CEO Antonio Filosa. Since taking over from Carlos Tavares, Filosa is focused on reviving Stellantis’ North American market by bringing back the Jeep Cherokee and altering the corporate structure to allow divisions in Europe, South America, and North America to operate more autonomously from the parent company.

Since the beginning of the year, Stellantis (NYSE:STLA) shares have dropped 28% versus rivals Ford (F) and General Motors (GM) which are up 19% and 11% this year, respectively.

data.title: Nasdaq sets week-long record closing streak, S&P and Dow stall as Fed decision looms
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages closed mixed on Friday, with the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closing at new highs every day of the week, while the Dow (DJI) retreated from 46k level.

Now investors await the Federal Reserve's decision on interest rate cuts, with the markets pricing odds of a 25-basis-point rate cut next week at 94.4%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool. 

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) set a fifth consecutive record closing of 22,141.10 points, ending +0.4%. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) broke its winning streak and closed -0.1%, and the Dow (DJI) finished -0.6%.

Week-to-date, the indices were +2%, +1.6%, and +1%, respectively.

“This week, all three major indices closed bright green, despite dire protestations of ‘but the economy’ and ‘but seasonality’ from those people committed to trading the market that lives in their heads, as opposed to the actual market in front of them,” Seeking Alpha analyst Alex King of Cestrian Capital Research said.

Earlier, the U.S. Consumer Sentiment Index fell to 55.4 in September from 58.2 in August, missing the 54.9 consensus.

“Retail overconfidence is beginning to rear its head, yes, but bull markets don’t tend to dump at the first sign of euphoria; they dump when naysayers have become reluctant longs. That’s when there are no more buyers. Right now? There are plenty of naysayers yet to become buyers. Higher for longer,” King said.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.07%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis point to 3.57%.

Among market movers, the cryptocurrency exchange Gemini Space Station (GEMI) opened as much as +45% its IPO price, closing +15.5%, lifting the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND).

Other top gainers on Friday included Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) and Paramount Skydance (PSKY), which were +16.3% and +7.2% respectively on the announcement that the latter is preparing a majority cash bid for the entertainment giant.

U.S. stocks set another record closing on Thursday, with the Dow Jones (DJI) reaching the 46,000 level for the first time, after the consumer inflation report came higher on a monthly basis in August, while core CPI was steady.

More on markets:

data.title: Apple AI executive plans to exit company next month: report
data.description:
Robby Walker, one of Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) senior artificial intelligence executives, plans to leave the company next month, according to a report by Bloomberg.

Walker leads Apple's Answers, Knowledge and Information teams, which is within Apple's Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning organization. He has worked at Apple since 2013, when the Cupertino-based company acquired his startup Cue, which created a personal search and proactive assistance tool. His first startup, Zenter, was acquired by Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) in 2007.

Walker was most recently working on Apple's new AI-powered web search system, according to the report, which cited people familiar with the situation.

If true, his departure would drive up the number of high-profile AI researchers who have left Apple over the last few months. Researcher Jian Zhang, who had worked at Apple for a decade, including most recently as the head of robotics research, left earlier this month to take a similar position at Meta Platforms (META).

Other Apple employees leaving over the past month include John Peebles, Nan Du and Zhao Meng. Ruoming Pang, who created Apple's AI models team, has also departed. AI engineers Tom Gunter, Mark Lee, Bowen Zhang and Yun Zhu all quit for Meta as well. Also, Frank Chu, who led Apple AI teams for cloud infrastructure, training, and search, has joined Meta's Superintelligence Labs to work on AI infrastructure.

data.title: Striking Boeing employees in Missouri turn down latest offer
data.description:
• Boeing (NYSE:BA) union workers who work on defense aircraft at several plants in Missouri have rejected the latest contract offer from the aerospace giant. 
  • The rebuff comes after management and IAM Union District 837 Negotiating Committee on Sept. 10 said they had come to a tentative, 5-year agreement that would have boosted the average wage to $109K from $75K, as well as a $4K ratification bonus.
  • A Boeing briefing sheet on the offer states that the new offer represents 45% average wage growth, a 5% increase from a prior offer made in late July.
  • The union told KMOV-TV that the latest offer's signing bonus wasn't as high as other workers have received, and lacked an increase in 401k benefits.
  • About 3,200 Boeing employees in Missouri began their strike on Aug. 4.

data.title: BlackRock exec Rick Rieder rises in contender rankings for Fed chair - report
data.description:
Rick Rieder, who oversees asset management giant BlackRock's (NYSE:BLK) fixed income business, is climbing in the the list of potential successors to Jerome Powell as Federal Reserve chair, according to a media report published Friday. 

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent interviewed Rieder for the role during a two-hour meeting in New York, discussing monetary policy, the Fed's structure, and regulatory oversight, Bloomberg reported, citing an administration official.

Bessent praised Rieder's extensive markets background, as well as his leadership of large investment teams, his grasp of both micro- and macroeconomic forces, and his strength in applying forward-looking frmaeworks to gauge the economy, a person familiar with Bessent's thinking told Bloomberg.

So far, Bessent has sat down with four of the 11 publicly identified contenders for the Fed chair role, and the roster is expected to grow by another one or two names, the administration official said, the article said.

It was reported Thursday that Bessent this week has been meeting with a few candidates on President Donald Trump's shortlist for the next Fed chair, including former Fed governors Lawrence Lindsey and Kevin Warsh, as well as former St. Louis Fed President James Bullard.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
